name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَن يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِنًا إِلَّا خَطَـًٔا ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَ مُؤْمِنًا خَطَـًٔا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ وَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن يَصَّدَّقُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَّكُمْ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِن قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ فَدِيَةٌ مُّسَلَّمَةٌ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ وَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُّؤْمِنَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ تَوْبَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٩٢)
```

```english
A believer may not kill another believer, unless it is by mistake. Anyone who kills a believer by mistake should set free a believing slave, and pay blood-money to his family, unless they remit it in charity. If he belongs to a people that are hostile to you but is a believer, then a believing slave is to be set free. And if he belongs to a people with whom you have a treaty, the blood-money is to be paid to his family and a believing slave is to be set free. He who cannot afford [to pay the blood-money], must fast two successive months as a penance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (92)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِنًا مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآؤُهُۥ جَهَنَّمُ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَلَعَنَهُۥ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُۥ عَذَابًا عَظِيمًا (٩٣)
```

```english
Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his requital shall be hell, to remain in it [forever]; Allah shall be wrathful at him and curse him and He shall prepare for him a great punishment. (93)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا۟ وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَنْ أَلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِنًا تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَغَانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ كُنتُم مِّن قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٩٤)
```

```english
O you who have faith! When you issue forth in the way of Allah, try to ascertain: do not say to someone who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer,’ seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world. Yet with Allah are plenteous gains. You too were such earlier, but Allah did you a favour. Therefore, do ascertain. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (94)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً** "It is not for a believer to kill a believer except by mistake" (4:92). The word "mistake" (الخطأ) — *an act committed without intent or correct judgment* — is pronounced with two short vowels and without elongation; with elongation, it follows the pattern of *fi‘āl*. It is the opposite of what is correct (الصواب). What is meant here is that which stands in contrast to intentionality (التعمد), as it is contrasted with the following verse: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً** "Should anyone kill a believer intentionally" (4:93).

The negation in His saying, "It is not for a believer to kill a believer," is a negation of requirement (نفي الاقتضاء). That is to say, there does not exist within a believer, after having entered the sanctuary and protection of faith (الإيمان), any requirement or inclination to kill a believer who is like him in that regard, regardless of the nature of the killing, except for accidental killing (قتل الخطإ). The exception here is connected (متصل), meaning the sense of the discourse returns to the fact that a believer does not desire to kill another believer *qua* believer—in that he would intend his death while knowing he is a believer. Similar to this sentence in its deployment for the negation of requirement are His sayings, the Exalted: **وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ** "It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him" (42:51); **مَا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَنْ تُنْبِتُوا شَجَرَهَا** "It was not for you to cause its trees to grow" (27:60); and **فَمَا كَانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِمَا كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** "But they would not believe what they had denied before" (10:74), among others.

Furthermore, the verse is phrased as a metonymy (كناية) for a legal prohibition (النهي التشريعي). This means that Allah the Exalted has never permitted, nor will He ever permit, a believer to kill a believer; He has forbidden it except in cases of accidental killing. Since there was no intent to kill a believer in such a case—either because the killing was not intended at all, or it was intended but under the assumption that the slain person was a disbeliever whose killing is permissible, for example—no prohibition is established there.

A group of exegetes mentioned that the exception in His saying, "except by mistake," is disconnected (منقطع). They argued that the phrase "except by mistake" was not treated as a literal exception because that would lead to the command or permission of accidental killing. However, you have already seen that this only leads to the removal of prohibition from accidental killing, or the non-placement of prohibition therein, and there is absolutely no problem in that. Thus, the truth is that the exception is connected (متصل).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ قَتَلَ مُؤْمِناً خَطَأً** — until His saying — **يَصَّدَّقُوا** "...and whoever kills a believer by mistake, [there shall be] the freeing of a believing slave and blood-money submitted to his family, unless they remit it as charity" (4:92). Freeing (التحرير) means making a slave free. The "neck" (الرقبة) refers to the physical neck, but it is commonly used as a metaphor (مجاز) for the enslaved person (النفس المملوكة). Blood-money (الدية) is the wealth given as compensation for a life, a limb, or otherwise. The meaning is: whoever kills a believer by accidental killing (قتل الخطإ), it is obligatory upon him to free an enslaved believing soul and to give blood-money, delivering it to the family of the deceased, unless the guardians of the slain person give the blood-money as charity (يتصدق) to the one who owes it and pardon him, in which case the blood-money is no longer obligatory.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَكُمْ** "If he belongs to a people who are your enemies" (4:92). The pronoun refers back to the slain believer. The "enemy people" (القوم العدو) are the combatant disbelievers (الكفار المحاربون). The meaning is: if the one killed by mistake was a believer but his family are combatant disbelievers who do not inherit, then freeing a slave is obligatory, but there is no blood-money, as a combatant disbeliever inherits nothing from a believer.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ مِيثاقٌ** "And if he belongs to a people with whom you have a treaty" (4:92). The pronoun in "he was" (كان) also refers to the slain believer, as indicated by the context. A treaty (الميثاق) — *a formal covenant or binding agreement* — is a general term for any pledge, encompassing the protection-covenant (الذمة) and every other pact. The meaning is: if the slain believer is from a people with whom you have a covenant, then blood-money and the freeing of a slave are obligatory. The blood-money is mentioned first here as an emphasis on observing the side of the treaty.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ** "But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast" (4:92). That is, whoever is unable to free a slave—as this is the closest referent in the text—must fast for two consecutive months.

His saying, the Exalted: **تَوْبَةً مِنَ اللهِ** "as a penance from Allah" (4:92). That is, this ruling—the obligation of fasting—is a turning (توبة) and an act of mercy from Allah toward the one who lacks a slave to free. This applies as a mitigation (التخفيف), so the ruling is a concession from Allah for the one who is unable. It is also possible that the word "penance" (توبة) is a qualifier referring to all the mentioned forms of expiation (الكفارة) in the verse, namely His saying: "the freeing of a slave," etc. The meaning would be that making expiation obligatory for the accidental killer is a turning and a grace from Allah for the killer regarding the stain that undoubtedly attached to him due to this act. It also serves to make him cautious so that he does not act recklessly in rushing to kill, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْقِصاصِ حَيَاةٌ** "There is life for you in retribution" (2:179).

Likewise, it is a turning from Allah for the community and a grace for them, as it adds one person to the ranks of the free after they have lost one, and it repairs the financial harm that befell the family of the deceased through the delivered blood-money.

From here it becomes apparent that Islam views freedom (الحرية) as life and enslavement (الاسترقاق) as a type of death; it also views the average benefit of an individual's existence as being represented by the full blood-money (الدية الكاملة). We shall clarify this meaning in the upcoming discussions.

As for determining the specific meanings of mistake (الخطأ), intent (العمد), freeing (التحرير), blood-money (الدية), the family of the deceased, the treaty (الميثاق), and other matters mentioned in the verse, this is referred to the Sunnah (السنة). Whoever wishes to investigate them should consult jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ** "Should anyone kill a believer intentionally, his ostensible reward shall be hell" (4:93). Intentionality (التعمد) is the aiming for an act under the specific description (العنوان) that belongs to it. Since a voluntary act is never devoid of an intended description, and it is possible for one act to have more than one description, a single act can be intentional from one perspective and accidental from another. For instance, one who shoots at a silhouette believing it to be game, when in reality it is a human being, has intended to hunt but has acted accidentally in killing the human. Similarly, if one strikes a person with a staff intending to discipline him, but the blow kills him, the killing is accidental. Based on this, the one who kills a believer intentionally is he who intends by his act the killing of the believer, while knowing that it is a killing and that the slain person is a believer.

Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, has spoken severely regarding the threat to the intentional killer of a believer with eternal Fire. However, you have already learned in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him" (4:48), that this verse, as well as His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً** "Indeed Allah forgives all sins" (39:53), are capable of qualifying this verse. Thus, while this verse threatens with eternal Fire, it is not explicit in its inevitability (الحتم); therefore, pardon is possible through repentance (التوبة) or intercession (الشفاعة).

His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَتَبَيَّنُوا** "O you who have faith! When you go forth in the way of Allah, distinguish" (4:94). "Going forth" (الضرب) refers to traveling through the land. Its qualification by "in the way of Allah" indicates that what is meant is going out for Jihad (الجهاد). "Distinguishing" (التبين) means differentiation, and what is meant is differentiating between the believer and the disbeliever, as evidenced by His saying: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقى إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِناً** "and do not say to him who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer’" (4:94). Offering peace (إلقاء السلام) refers to offering the greeting—the greeting of the people of faith. It has also been recited as: "to him who offers you submission (السلم)" with a short vowel on the *lām*, meaning surrender.

What is meant by "seeking the goods of the life of this world" (ابتغاء عرض الحياة الدنيا) is the pursuit of wealth and spoils (الغنيمة). His saying: **فَعِنْدَ اللهِ مَغانِمُ كَثِيرَةٌ** "with Allah are plenteous spoils" (4:94). The word "spoils" (مغانم) is the plural of *magnam*, meaning booty. That is, the spoils with Allah are better than the spoils of this world which they desire, due to their abundance and permanence; therefore, these are what you must prefer.

His saying, the Exalted: **كَذلِكَ كُنْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَمَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَتَبَيَّنُوا** "You too were such before, but Allah beckoned to you; so distinguish" (4:94). That is, you were upon this description—seeking the goods of the life of this world—before you believed. Then Allah graced you with faith, which turns you away from seeking the goods of the life of this world toward the plenteous spoils that are with Allah. Since this is the case, it is obligatory upon you to distinguish. The repetition of the command to distinguish serves as an emphasis of the ruling.

The verse, while containing an exhortation and a type of reproach, does not explicitly state that this killing—which it appears occurred—was the intentional killing of a believer. It seems that it was an accidental killing by some believers of some polytheists who offered peace, because the killer did not trust that he was truly a believer, assuming he only displayed faith out of fear for his life. The verse reproaches him by stating that Islam only considers the outward (الظاهر), and leaves the matter of the hearts to the Subtle, the All-Aware.

Accordingly, His saying: "seeking the goods of the life of this world" is placed in the discourse based on the requirement of the situation (اقتضاء الحال). That is, your situation in killing one who displays faith to you, without paying heed to his case or distinguishing his state, is the situation of one who desires wealth and spoils; such a person kills the believer who outwardly professes faith with the slightest excuse, without it being a valid justification. This was the state the believers were in before their faith—seeking nothing but the world. Thus, when Allah blessed them with faith and graced them with Islam, it became obligatory for them to distinguish in what they do and not to be led by the character of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) or the remnants of its impulses within them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthūr*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً** (the verse): Ibn Jarir narrated from ‘Ikrimah who said: "Al-Harith ibn Yazid ibn Nabishah, from the tribe of ‘Amir ibn Lu’ayy, used to torture ‘Ayyash ibn Abi Rabi‘ah along with Abu Jahl. Later, he [Al-Harith] went out as a migrant to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. ‘Ayyash met him at al-Harrah and struck him down with a sword, thinking he was still a disbeliever. He then came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him, so the verse was revealed: **وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ أَنْ يَقْتُلَ مُؤْمِناً إِلَّا خَطَأً**. The Prophet read it to him and then said: 'Stand up and free [a slave].'"

I say: This meaning has been narrated through other chains as well. In some of them, it is mentioned that he killed him in Mecca on the day of the Conquest, when ‘Ayyash came out—having been in the bonds of the polytheists until that day while they tortured him—and met Harith, who had accepted Islam, but ‘Ayyash did not know of his Islam, so he killed him then. What we have established from the narration of ‘Ikrimah is more consistent with reason and more appropriate to the chronology of the revelation of Surah al-Nisa’.

Al-Tabari narrated in his *Tafsir* from Ibn Zayd: "The one concerning whom the verse was revealed was Abu al-Darda’. He was in a raiding party and turned into a mountain pass for a personal need. He found a man from the [enemy] people with some sheep. He attacked him with a sword, and the man said, 'There is no god but Allah.' He struck him anyway, then brought his sheep to the party. Later, he felt something in his soul, so he came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him, and the verse was revealed."

It is also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* from al-Ruyani, Ibn Mandah, and Abu Nu‘aym, from Bakr ibn Harithah al-Juhani: "He was the one concerning whom the verse was revealed, due to a story similar to that of Abu al-Darda’." In any case, the narrations do no more than provide applications (التطبيق) of the verse.

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from his men, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Every manumission is permissible for a newborn except in the expiation for killing, for Allah the Exalted says: **فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مُؤْمِنَةٍ** (the freeing of a believing slave)—meaning by that one who professes [faith] and has reached the age of puberty (بلغت الحنث).'" (The hadith).

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from Musa ibn Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: He was asked, "How is a believing woman recognized?" He said: "By the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*."

In *al-Faqih*, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: Regarding a Muslim man in the land of polytheism whom the Muslims killed, and the Imam later learned of it, he عليه‌السلام said: "He shall free a believing slave in his place, and that is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **فَإِنْ كَانَ مِنْ قَوْمٍ عَدُوٍّ لَكُمْ** (If he belongs to a people who are your enemies)."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the same.

In his saying, "He shall free in his place," there is an indication that the reality of manumission is the addition of one person to the free Muslims, since one was subtracted from their number, as previously indicated.

Perhaps it can be inferred from this that the benefit of manumission in general for expiations is the addition of a free, non-disobedient person to their number, since one of them was subtracted by the sin. Understand this.

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "If a man is obligated to fast two consecutive months and he breaks the fast or falls ill in the first month, he must restart the fast. But if he fasts the first month and some of the second month, and then something occurs for which he has an excuse, he only has to complete what remains."

I say: That is, he completes what remains of it, as has been said, and this is inferred from the requirement of "consecutive" (التتابع).

In *al-Kafi* and *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from him عليه‌السلام: He was asked about a believer who kills a believer intentionally—does he have any repentance? He said: "If he killed him for his faith, he has no repentance. But if he killed him out of anger or for some worldly reason, then his repentance is that he be subjected to retaliation (يقاد منه). If he was not caught, he should go to the guardians of the slain, confess to them the killing of their companion; if they pardon him and do not kill him, he gives them the blood-money, frees a soul, fasts two consecutive months, and feeds sixty poor people as a repentance to Allah, Mighty and Majestic."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to Abu al-Safatij from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ**, he said: "His reward is Hell *if* He rewards him."

I say: This meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* from al-Tabarani and others, from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The narrations, as you see, contain the subtle points of the verses we previously mentioned. In the chapter of killing and retaliation (القود), there are many narrations; whoever desires them should consult the compendiums of hadith.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ** (the verse), it is said: "It was revealed concerning Miqyas ibn Dubabah al-Kinani. He found his brother Hisham slain among the Banu al-Najjar, so he mentioned that to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The Prophet sent Qays ibn Hilal al-Fihri with him and said to him: 'Tell the Banu al-Najjar: If you know the killer of Hisham, hand him over to his brother to take retaliation; if you do not know, then hand over his blood-money to him.' Al-Fihri delivered the message, and they gave him the blood-money. When he departed with al-Fihri, Satan whispered to him, saying: 'You have done nothing; you took the blood-money for your brother and it will be a disgrace upon you. Kill the one with you so it will be a life for a life, and the blood-money will be an extra gain.' So he struck him with a rock, killed him, rode a camel, and returned to Mecca as a disbeliever, reciting:

*I killed Fihri in his stead and placed his blood-money*
*Upon the chiefs of Banu al-Najjar, the masters of Fari‘*
*Thus I attained my vengeance and lay down pillowed*
*And I was the first to return to the idols.*

The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله then said: 'I do not grant him safety in the profane land nor the sanctuary.'" This was narrated by al-Dahhak and a group of exegetes. (End).

I say: Something similar was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, and others.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ** (the verse), it is said that it was revealed when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله returned from the expedition of Khaybar and sent Usamah ibn Zayd with cavalry to some Jewish villages in the direction of Fadak to invite them to Islam. There was a man called Mirdas ibn Nahik al-Fadaki in one of the villages. When he sensed the cavalry of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he gathered his family and wealth in a part of the mountain and began saying, "I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah." Usamah ibn Zayd passed by him and stabbed him, killing him. When he returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and informed him of that, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: "Did you kill a man who bore witness that there is no god but Allah and that I am the Messenger of Allah?" He replied: "O Messenger of Allah, he only said it to seek refuge from being killed." The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "You did not uncover the veil from his heart, nor did you accept what he said with his tongue, nor did you know what was in his soul." After that, Usamah swore that he would never kill anyone who bore witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; thus, he held back from [supporting] the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام in his wars. Regarding this, the verse was revealed: **وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقى إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِناً تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** (the verse).

I say: Al-Tabari narrated this meaning in his *Tafsir* from al-Suddi. Many narrations are found in *al-Durr al-Manthūr* regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse. In some, the story is attributed to Miqdad ibn al-Aswad; in others, to Abu al-Darda’; in others, to Muhallim ibn Jaththamah; and in some, the names of the killer and the slain are not mentioned, leaving the story ambiguous. However, the oath of Usamah ibn Zayd and his excuse to ‘Ali عليه‌السلام for holding back from his wars is well-known and mentioned in history books. And Allah knows best.
